speaker: interviewer
text: Tape seven. The liberator is Edward Ruston.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a picture of Chatteris in Cambridgeshire. This is a picture of the parish church, which is really the focal center of the town. And on the right-hand side of the picture is of the war memorial to the town's dead in World War I and World War II. And on the 11th of November at 11 o'clock in the morning, it was here where the townspeople, when I was a boy, would muster and carry out certain functions, namely reading the oration-- they shall not grow old as we that are left grow old, age shall not weary them, nor the years condemn. At the going down of the sun and in the morning, we will remember them. And everybody who's assembled repeated the last words. And there's some 200 names on that war memorial, including my brothers'. And then they would go into the parish church. It mattered not what their religions was. And they would worship, as a corporate body, the town mourning its dead. This is a picture of Headmaster of King Edward School, Mr. A.J. Kidd. And this is the man who I said is-- compares very favorably with Dr. Arnold of Rugby School, the great educationalist of the 19th century in England. This gentleman used to take us boys in the senior class-- that would be the 13-year-olds-- and we each had a plot of land. And we cultivated it. And he really was an absolute expert. He knew full well that most of us being-- living in a farming area would finish up on the land. And he took great interest in getting us interested in the land, and gardens, and food production. In fact, my plot was plot number seven. And believe it or believe it not, donkey's years before, my uncle, who was also named Ted, actually had the same plot. This picture is a picture of the 451st mountain battery, in its mountain role, sharing the mules, carrying pack gun loads. It was taken in North Wales, in Snowdonia, what is now the national park called the Devil's Steps. Now, the-- these two mules are each carrying a part of the gun barrel. The-- the front mule is carrying the front end, which is called the chase. And the second mule is carrying the rear end, the breech end. And, of course, the great asset of this gun was it broke down into mule loads. And we exploited that when we went to war. Because we put it into submarines. We put it into landing craft. Any track vehicle, any wheel vehicle we could get our hands on. And indeed, we even man-packed it. But that's a picture showing the men in the-- the-- what there's-- what was their normal dress-- [INAUDIBLE] pullovers, steel helmets, britches, and [INAUDIBLE]. It was a great outfit. This picture is a painting by Captain Vincent Cohen, who was, in fact, the troop commander of this troop of 452 mountain battery, also in first mountain regiment. And the story behind this picture is that this mountain gun, which you saw on mules in a picture or two before, was supporting the infantry during the attack on Vlissingen-- also called Flushing-- on the first of November, 1944. And the-- the attacking infantry, which was in the Scottish 52nd division, were held up by the enemy, who were holding a concrete pillbox the other side of the road from this room where this gun is in action, firing out the bedroom window. The-- Vincent Cohen, under his men, thought up the idea that if they couldn't get at this pillbox over the top of the houses, because the range was so short, the best they could do was take [INAUDIBLE] open sites. So they [INAUDIBLE] this house. And they decided to dismantle the gun, which of course you can do with a mountain howitzer. And they carried the bits up the stairs and deployed it in the bedroom. The-- some of these bits weigh 400 pounds. And it was a-- an enormous job to get this gun up the staircase. Now, the number one of the gun is the chap who's hunched looking down the barrel. His name was Sergeant Walker. And in actual fact, he stayed with us over Easter this past year, in 1996. And he was telling me how he chose this gun platform. And they took the mattress off the bed and put it up to the window so as the Germans on the other side of the road-- and it really is the other side of the road-- couldn't see what they were up to. And then pull the mattress away and fired out of the bedroom window. They fired a couple of rounds. And then the Germans came out. And by that time the spades, which you can see sticking up at the end of the trail, the spades were already through the ceiling into the room below. I think, one of the stoutest efforts I've ever heard of. A lot of initiative. and damn brave chaps. And it was a pleasure to have him in the house. And as he left to go back to Scotland, where he now lives, I said to him, in a joke, well, don't forget, if you ever have to do this again, try to avoid setting fire to the curtains. You'll see in this picture the curtains are on fire. These are photographs of my actual identity medals, which are-- my identity disks, rather, which I wore in battle. They're threaded together in a certain order, so that if you-- bells of hell go ting-a-ling-a-ling for you, you can slip the bottom disk off the string without taking the assembly off the dead person's neck. So that the one which has the two holes punched in it is left on the body for identity when the gravediggers go along, the burial parties go along, and deal with whatever they have to deal. And that's a system which was used in the First World War. But in the First World War, and at the beginning of the Second World War, we used to use fiber-- fiberboard disks. But by the time I went back, we were using these metal ones. And you'll see they have on them the number-- 140218-- if you can read it, it's C in the middle-- church of England. And you were that if you were nothing else. Because you wasn't allowed to die and be nothing. And then, the name at the bottom, Ruston, E.W. Thank you. This is my green beret, which has got a history attached to it. It is my green beret. It was presented to me by Brigadier Mills Roberts. On the Rhine crossing, where I was wearing a steel helmet, it was a flaming nuisance. It got in the way of my headgear on my wireless set. And I-- I said out aloud, this steel helmet is a bloody nuisance. And the brigade major, who was standing alongside me, said, what you need is a bereted head. And the Brigadier said, good. Good idea. I'll give you one. You shall have one. And that's how I got it. And the badge is the badge of the Royal Regiment of Artillery. You'll see that it's got [INAUDIBLE] in the scroll below it and the straight flames out of the top of the grenade. If it was a sapper, there would be little flames in front of it. But I actually wore that hat in battle. I wore it when we seized Osnabruck. I wore it when we crossed the River Weser and seized the German Sarin gas factory people. I wore it on the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I wore it again on the Elbe crossing at Lauenburg. And indeed, I wore it in Neustadt. And all that time, I had an ordinary bronze badge in the top of it. And when I came back to England, my Brigadier said to me, Ted, go and get yourself a decent gold braided badge, and put it on my account. I did just that. He paid for it. This is a photograph taken of me in Neustadt, by a chap named [INAUDIBLE], who was a photographer in the town. And it was taken, really, for my wife. It shows me wearing my green beret. I suppose that I would be about 30 at that time. And it shows the crown, which is my badge of rank. Below that, there is the Royal Artillery flash. And below that on my arm, there, is the national flag of Scotland-- it's an Andrew's cross-- and below that was the-- in the scroll, the word mountain. Now, these were all signs peculiar to the 52nd Mountain Scottish Division. Now, people may wonder if the Commandos had a badge of their own. They did not. There was no badge for the-- cap badge-- for the Commandos. They wore a shoulder flash which was combined operations. But everybody who served with the Commandos-- who passed and was acceptable-- got-- wore a green beret. And on the green beret, he wore the cap badge of his regiment. And I was a gunner, the Royal Artillery. And that's a gunner badge. This is a notable picture. It is, in fact, a copy produced by the Illustrated London Newspaper, for me, and given to me by them. It is in fact a copy of the war artist [NON-ENGLISH] who did this drawing of first Commando brigade crossing the river Rhine in the assault at Wesel, 23rd of March, 1945. The sappers blew a hole in our bank. We were crossing to the western bank. And then we went down the mud and across the river. It was about 400 yards wide and a five-knot tide. And the Germans were engaging these Buffalo craft with small arms from their positions on the other side of the river. I crossed in the first flight with the Brigadier. And we made a safe landing. And eventually, on this window display, we will show you a picture of a piece of stone, which was where I landed. The-- these track vehicles were American vehicles called Buffalos. And they were most remarkably efficient. They were not armored. And it was as well not to think too much about that when you were crossing one of these wide rivers and you were being shot at. They were tracked. And they were-- they would carry 25 men, or a gun, or a jeep. And they were a superb vehicle for the job. And all part of the Americans who built them. This is a picture of our final parade in Neustadt camp, before we left Germany to return to the Highlands, which I've already talked about. The-- this is the battery flag which is flying at the mast. And this is 451 battery, which I was privileged to command, lined up behind on that parade. It was in this building where I found people lying in excreta, white excreta, which I've never been able to explain or understand. It was dreadful. Outside, the right hand of the two doors, there were people lying there, dead and dying. And the wooden hut, which you can see-- between the flagstaff and the right-hand corner of that garage building, you'll see a wooden hut. Now that was the wooden hut where we organized the clothing issues and where my battery captain, at the critical moment, brought complete order out of utter chaos. This is a most unusual photograph. It is, in fact, a photograph copy of a photo which was owned by one of the six soldiers. And I think it was chap [INAUDIBLE], is the second from the right. His name is Turner. He was one of my signalers. And of course, all six of them were with me at Neustadt Camp. Now, this is a battle [INAUDIBLE] sign of the Kriegsmarine. And undoubtedly, they liberated it at Neustadt. And it is my belief, it is probably the flag which was used by the German Naval staff to fly on their flagstaff, which I had moved for the purpose of parade and which you've already seen in an earlier photograph. They are six great chaps. This is of particular interest to me. Because it is a piece of river rock taken from the banks of the River Rhine, at Wesel, in Germany, at the exact spot where we jumped ashore from the Buffalos, during the assault across the Rhine on the 23rd of March. The story on this particular specimen is that one of my troop commanders-- who was subsequently awarded the military cross for countless acts of bravery-- he did a visit with his son, who was anxious to drive his dad across the route which he followed from the French coast to Neustadt. And I got roped into this because the-- his father, who was very badly wounded in the last couple of days of the war, wasn't too clear about various bits and pieces. So he asked me to map out the course. And I had a lot of fun. And-- but we did. We mapped the course out, the way he should go and the way he should come back. And, course, we had no idea whether the bridges were working or not. But anyway, we did it for him. And he very kindly picked up this piece of stone, stuck it in his pocket, and when he got back, he got this piece of stone mounted on this block of marble and put the little silver plate on it commemorating the fact what it was all about. I-- I treasure it greatly. This silver-gilt medallion was given to me by the Norwegian police officers whom we liberated at Neustadt in 1945, and who did such a good job helping me to control the spread of typhus by taking on work, which physically, they really shouldn't have done. On the other side of the medallion, there is the picture of a blindfolded justice lady-- the-- the angel of justice, or whatever they call them. It stands normally on a block of Norwegian crystal. But we keep the two separate because as yet, we haven't found a way in which we can keep this stuck onto the crystal. None of the modern glues will do it. These are my war medals. The little round disk at the top is the the official badge of the Army Commandos Old Comrades Association. The-- in the center is the Wilkinson fighting knife. And round the top, you have Commandos, united we conquer. That was their badge. The medals, well, they're very much the same as most people got. The one on the left is a military cross, which was actually hung on my chest by the King at Holyrood Palace in 1945. And the others are campaigns, in 1939, '45 the France and Germany star, the defense medal, the war medal, and then at the very end, with the Cyprus badge on it, is the general Service Medal. That was really tied up with my service in Cyprus at the time of Suez. That particular amount is called a court mount. And in a picture which you will see of me in a minute in my robes as a County Borough Council of the borough Bournemouth, they are in just hanging order. This is a more expensive way. This is a picture of myself in the ceremonial robes of a county borough councilor of the county borough of Bournemouth [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in England. I was elected to that council in 1970. This picture would be vintage 1970, '71. The ceremonial robe is a deep blue with black facings. And the medals, as I said, just ordinary-- no special order, not court dress, as indeed, when they were shown on their own. The-- it was a most interesting time. Because the government in London had decided it was going to get rid of we county boroughs. We fought like hell to stop the stupid government-- which was conservative, I must say-- doing it. That-- they refused to listen. They disbanded the county boroughs. And in actual fact, April next year, they are reconstituting the county boroughs. 25 years, a very sad failure. But at least, better late than never. This is a photograph of my wife. It was taken by some very dear Australian friends of ours at Mount Macedon, two years ago. We-- at that time, we had been married about 50 years. We're still happily married. Mustn't say anymore or else I'll get into trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say what year it was, too, [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken, this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it'd be '94. '94, '95. This is the official invitation my wife, Sally, our daughter, and I received, to go to a garden party at Buckingham Palace in 1984. This is, in fact, quite an interesting occasion. Because, as you can see from the invitation card, the Queen Mother, Queen Elizabeth, and the Prince of Wales, will be present on the Queen's behalf. At that time, the Queen was going to Canada. But the prime minister of Canada-- I think it was Pierre Trudeau-- called a snap election. And the Constitution does not permit the Queen to be in Canada at the time of the general election.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in actual fact, when we got to Buckingham Palace, we were met by one of the court gentleman, who handed us another card, which said that because of the election in Canada, the Queen would also be present. So we actually finished up at this garden party with her majesty the Queen, her mother, the Queen Mother, the Prince of Wales, Princess Anne, now Princess Royal, and a host of others, but none of the ladies who have been making public fame-- or infamy-- of recent date. Did you get to meet any of these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, close as you. But-- very close, but not singled out. This was our third invitation. We've actually been to Buckingham Palace twice. The previous time we went, we took our daughter Jane. At that time, Sally was in Canada. And very foolishly, I didn't give her the opportunity. Because you can only take unmarried daughters. And I thought she was in Canada and she wouldn't want to pay all the fares to come back. I was left in no doubt that I'd done the wrong thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- then somebody in Buckingham Palace heard about this. And they said, well, we-- we should invite Sally. And so, Sally got-- this is really a special invitation for Sally. But she could only get in like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, it's-- it's a very pleasant experience. Very pleasant experience. There's about 5,000 people there, so obviously everybody can't meet them. This picture evokes many memories for me. This is our granddaughter, [? Catherine. ?] It was taken in 1988. And in July of that year, I suffered a stroke. And I really wasn't worth very much. But by October, I had recovered sufficiently to carry out my public duties with the Christchurch borough council, of which I was a-- a-- a leading chairman. And our daughter, Jane, brought [? Catherine ?] to England, to come and see me. And she was there for the Remembrance Day service in November 1988. She was certainly the youngest one there in the front of the old priory church, which is 900 years old and only just had its 900th birthday. Just think of that-- 900 years old. And she sat in the church along with my wife and daughter. Now, the child actually made the colorful container for it. I think I'm very fortunate-- A, to be around here now to tell you gentlemen what happened 1988, and, to have her as a granddaughter.
